details.text: Derivative: الْمُرْسَلَاتِ: مُرْسَل is an Ism Maf’ool (اِسْمُ المَفْعُول – object of the verb) from أَرْسَلَ – يُرْسِلُ – إِرْسَال which means “to send or release”. مُرْسَلَات (Mursalaat) is the feminine plural and is only used in general when you are talking about non-human things. It is usually used to describe something but the thing that it is describing has not been mentioned. As such there are a variety of interpretations for what it is referring to. One of those opinions is that الْمُرْسَلَاتِ (Al-Mursalaat) is referring to the winds which have been sent out or released by Allah.

- عُرْفًا is the حال (Haal – condition) of الْمُرْسَلَاتِ (Al-Mursalaat). عُرْف (‘Urf) is used to refer to the hair at the back of the horse or its mane which is used to pull a wild horse back or to control it when riding acting in place of a rein. When الْمُرْسَلَاتِ (Al-Mursalaat i.e., winds) are released and their reins (عُرْف) are let go and released, they go out of control. The scholars of Tafsir have also stated that وَالْمُرْسَلَاتِ عُرْفًا means the winds that come one after another (in gusts) in the case where the mane or hair of the horse (عُرْف) can be described as being layered, one layer of hair on another.
Derivative: نُسِفَتْ from نَسَفَ and it means to be plucked out from its roots or to be uprooted. It can also imply to be pulverised or turned to dust.

Derivative: أُقِّتَتْ from أَقَّتَ is the same as وُقِّتَتْ from وَقَّتَ as the وَاو (Waw – و) and the هَمْزَة (Hamzah – أ) are interchangeable with this verb. Its meaning is to set a time for something to happen i.e., to schedule something.
Derivative: أُجِّلَتْ from أَجَّلَ which means to delay, hold back or defer.

Derivative: الْفَصْلِ is from فَصَلَ “The separation”. فَصْلٌ (Fasl) means “separation”. The Day of Al-Fasl (يَوْمُ الْفَصْلِ) is the day in which the truth will be separated from falsehood, and the believers from the disbelievers. فَصْلٌ (Fasl) also means “decisive and final”.
Derivative: وَيْلٌ means: "What terrible destruction (إهْلاك)”

Derivative: الْعَاصِفَاتِ – Originally عَصْف (‘Asf) refers to “inconsiderate motion” i.e., movement that does not give consideration to what may be in the way. الْعَاصِفَاتِ (Al-‘Aasifaat) refers to winds that blow in a manner that do not care what’s in their path.

فَالْعَاصِفَاتِ عَصْفًا

- عَصْفًا is the Haal (condition) of الْعَاصِفَاتِ and as such the meaning is that “Winds that blow with absolutely no consideration of what lies in their path”, the added عَصْفًا gives greater emphasis to how violent and inconsiderate they are.
Derivative: مَّهِينٍ is something humiliating, weak and regarded with contempt.
Derivative: قَرَارٍ is a place where something settles.
Derivative: مَكِين means secure and strongly fortified.
Derivative: كِفَاتًا refers to a container, something that contains something, holds and gathers it within its containment. Here it is in reference to the earth which contains all life within it.
Derivative: فُرَاتًا means sweet and fresh in reference to water.
Derivative: شَامِخَاتٍ: شَامِخ means lofty and towering.

وَالنَّاشِرَاتِ نَشْرًا

- This is a new Qasm (oath) that Allah is swearing by. It means: And I swear (وَ) by the spreaders (النَّاشِرَاتِ) that spread far and wide (نَشْرًا)”. Here, Allah is swearing by another kind of wind, the spreading winds that spread far and wide i.e., spreading rain clouds to different places and spreading seeds across the earth.
Derivative: ظِلٍّ the ظِلّ (Zhill – Shade) here refers to smoke and not a shade that shades you from the heat.
Derivative: ظَلِيلٍ is shade that protect someone from the heat
Derivative: شَرَرٍ means sparks but refers here to that which flies out from fire and flames which are tremendously larger than what we are familiar with in terms of sparks.
Derivative: الْقَصْرِ means a castle or fortress and can also refer to its domes due to be the visual part of it from a distance, or to something the size of a towering building.
- It literally means “Yellow camels” however the Arabs used this phrase to refer to the black camels. The image is therefore one of blackness.
Derivative: يَنطِقُونَ from نَطَقَ which means to utter or pronounce a word i.e., they will not utter a word or sound.

فَالْفَارِقَاتِ فَرْقًا

- It literally means: “The separating (winds) that separate (in a precise manner)”. In continuation of the previous verses, it refers to the winds that separate, allocate and divide what they spread in terms of clouds and seeds. It is derived from فَرَقَ which means to divide.
Derivative: فَوَاكِهَ means “Fruits” and comes from فَكِهَ which means to joyful and happy. فَوَاكِهَ are those fruits that bring you joy and put a smile on your face.
Derivative: هَنِيئًا means in a state of ease, comfort, enjoyment and satisfaction, without any concerns or constraints in respect to satisfying oneself.

فَالْمُلْقِيَاتِ ذِكْرًا

- Here the winds are being described as those which “Throw down or bring (people) into contact with a reminder”. The winds which are constantly throwing (مُلْقِيَاتِ) a reminder (ذِكْرًا). مُلْقِي (Mulqi) is from the verb أَلْقَى – يُلْقِي – إِلْقَاء which means to throw down.
Derivative: حَدِيثٍ means speech and can imply something that is knew, like speech that has not been heard before.
Derivative: نُذْرًا – نُذْرٌ (Nudhr) means warning. نُذْرًا here implies the meaning that the winds are enough of a warning.

Derivative: عُذْرًا - عُذْرٌ (‘Udhr) means an excuse. عُذْرًا here implies the meaning that the winds are enough of an excuse” i.e., it is sufficient enough as a justification to condemn the ones who denies the signs of the wind.

إِنَّمَا تُوعَدُونَ لَوَاقِعٌ

- Literally means: “No doubt (إِنَّ), what (مَا) you have been promised (تُوعَدُونَ) is bound to occur or is definitely going to happen (لَوَاقِعٌ)”. This statement is the جَوابُ القَسْم (Jawaab ul-Qasm – The answer to the oaths which were sworn by the winds which were mentioned in the previous verses).
Derivative: طُمِسَتْ from طَمَسَ which means to erase or blot out. طُمِسَتْ means to be erased or blotted out.
Derivative: فُرِجَتْ from فَرَجَ – فَرْجًا which means to split, torn or cleaved open or apart.
